text: To stick in your mind
A landmark
A well-kept secret
To stumble upon
Unassuming
Weathered
Bustling
Sleepy
To grow on you
A haven
To leave a mark on
To be steeped in history
It is the town that stayed with me.
The cafe where we met is still there.
A woman who ran the cafe waved me in.
Bergen, which sits between mountains, is stunning.
So this is the square that I told you about. That weathered old house over there, which is nearly three hundred years old, is where I grew up.
It is beautiful. And that little shop on the corner, the one whose windows are full of bread?
That is the bakery that everyone loves. The baker, who has worked there for fifty years, still remembers every name. This is a sleepy place, but it grows on you.
I can see why it stayed with you. It is so unassuming, and yet it is steeped in history.
Exactly. The tourists all go to the city that has the famous cathedral. But this is the place that I would never trade. It is my haven.
Back home I have a street like this, a narrow one whose cafes I have known since I was a boy. Some places just leave a mark on you.
It is a place that stayed with me for years.
It is a town where I felt at home.
The people who live there made the trip.
It is a quiet place that grows on you.
It is an old harbor whose light I will never forget.

Hi Marcelo, it is Sigrid. You asked me which place I would go back to tomorrow if I could, and I have thought about it all day. It is not a city you would find in a guidebook. It is a tiny village on the west coast, whose harbor is so small that only three boats fit. There is a wooden house there, which my grandmother was born in, where the whole family still gathers every summer. And there is a baker, an old man who has run the same shop for fifty years, who still remembers the name of everyone who walks in. That is the place that stayed with me. Not because of what is there, but because of who is there. Come and see it one day. Bye now.

Welcome back to the show. Today, the places that refuse to leave us, and why they are so rarely the famous ones. Here is something every traveler eventually notices. You visit the great cities, the ones that are printed on every postcard, and somehow they blur together. But then there is that one small town whose name nobody recognizes, the little square where you sat for an hour, the cafe whose owner poured you a second coffee for free. Those are the places that stay. And it is worth asking why. It is almost never the scenery. It is the people who were there, the stranger who gave you directions, the family who invited you in. A place becomes unforgettable not because of what is built there, but because of who you met, and how they made you feel. So the next time a place stays with you, look closely. Odds are there is a face behind it.
